metadata.split_role: Expanded Suillus and Leccinum/scaber-stalk bolete records.

records.culinary_uses: Hold as an ID/taxonomy review record rather than a food target.

mushroom_profile.summary: Suillus punctatipes record kept separate from dotted-stalk Suillus until taxonomy/image review settles it.
mushroom_profile.season_note: August through October; treated as a late-summer into fall Suillus until local timing is better documented.
research_notes: Needs source links and fuller field notes.
Placeholder image inserted pending real public-usable photo.
Compare with Suillus punctipes and other white-pine Suillus: check pore color when young, glandular dots, host pine, cap slime, and any ring zone.
pore_color: Pore color must be checked young

records.mushroom_card_note: Suillus punctatipes record kept separate from dotted-stalk Suillus until taxonomy/image review settles it.
records.field_identification: Compare with Suillus punctipes and other white-pine Suillus: check pore color when young, glandular dots, host pine, cap slime, and any ring zone.
records.edibility_detail: Hold as an ID/taxonomy review record rather than a food target.

images_structured.title: Suillus punctatipes
images_structured.part_or_stage: whole fruiting body; exact species, not Suillus punctipes
images_structured.author: Dick Culbert
images_structured.credit: Dick Culbert, CC BY 2.0, via Wikimedia Commons
images_structured.rights_notes: Commercial use and modification allowed; attribution required.
images_structured.rights_note: Commercial use and modification allowed; attribution required.
images_structured.scope_note: Exact Suillus punctatipes image; range text should remain cautious if this record is retained for Upper Michigan.

records.culinary_uses: Edible by some accounts but slimy and modest quality; peel if desired and cook thoroughly.

mushroom_profile.summary: Dotted-stalk Suillus: eastern white pine associate with brown young pores and dense darkening glandular dots.
mushroom_profile.season_note: August through October with eastern white pine.
research_notes: Needs source links and fuller field notes.
Look under eastern white pine for a slimy yellow to yellow-brown cap, brown young pore surface, and a stem densely dotted with glandular spots that darken with age.
pore_color: Brown to orange-brown when young; yellowish to olive with age

records.edibility_detail: Edible reputation is modest; cook well and expect typical Suillus texture/slime issues.
records.field_identification: Look under eastern white pine for a slimy yellow to yellow-brown cap, brown young pore surface, and a stem densely dotted with glandular spots that darken with age.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Suillus punctipes
use_links.notes: Eastern white pine association, brown young pores, and glandular dots.

records.edibility_notes: Cooked food. Edible by some accounts but slimy and modest quality; peel if desired and cook thoroughly.

edible_use.notes: Edible by some accounts but slimy and modest quality; peel if desired and cook thoroughly.

records.mushroom_card_note: Dotted-stalk Suillus: eastern white pine associate with brown young pores and dense darkening glandular dots.

images_structured.title: Dotted-stalk Suillus cap, pore surface, and dotted stem
images_structured.author: Walt Sturgeon / Mycowalt

lookalike_risk.label: Moderate — Suillus ID, texture, tolerance, and bolete confusion
lookalike_risk.short_label: Moderate — Suillus ID, texture, tolerance, and bolete confusion
lookalike_risk.summary: Suillus risk is usually correct Suillus ID, host association, slimy cap/glandular dots/ring traits, condition, and personal tolerance — not a deadly twin.
required_checks: Confirm Suillus traits such as slime, ring, or glandular dots as appropriate
Reject old, soggy, buggy, mushy specimens
lookalike_risk.hard_rule: Do not collapse all Suillus into “slippery jack”; wrong host, wrong ring/dots, or poor condition means stop.

dangerous_lookalikes.risk: Poisonous / not recommended red-pored blue-staining bolete group.
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Red/orange pores or fast strong blue staining mean stop unless expert-confirmed.

target_traits: White/yellow pores without bitter Tylopilus posture
required_checks: Tiny nibble-and-spit only if you already know the mushroom is safe to taste
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Bitter taste means not food; one specimen can ruin the pan.

target_traits: Species-specific ring, glandular dots, host tree, and pore size
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Do not collapse all Suillus into one edible label.

records.culinary_uses: Not a food target in this guide until taxonomy and regional relevance are clearer.

mushroom_profile.summary: Suillus sinuspaulianus is keyed near glandular yellow/brown Suillus; needs source/image confirmation and may overlap older names.
research_notes: Needs source links and fuller field notes.
Placeholder image inserted pending real public-usable photo.
Check pine host, cap color, pore shape, glandular dots, and ring/false-veil traits. This record should be treated as taxonomy-sensitive.

records.mushroom_card_note: Suillus sinuspaulianus is keyed near glandular yellow/brown Suillus; needs source/image confirmation and may overlap older names.
records.field_identification: Check pine host, cap color, pore shape, glandular dots, and ring/false-veil traits. This record should be treated as taxonomy-sensitive.
records.edibility_detail: Not a food target in this guide until taxonomy and regional relevance are clearer.

records.culinary_uses: This Suillus still needs fuller culinary and host-tree notes.

mushroom_profile.summary: Showy tamarack/larch Suillus with sticky red cap, grayish patches, red gelatinous veil, and a sticky ring.
mushroom_profile.season_note: August through October; late-summer into fall Suillus with larch/tamarack or related conifer habitat.
research_notes: Needs source links and fuller field notes.
Placeholder image inserted pending real public-usable photo.
Look with tamarack/larch for a sticky red cap with grayish patches, gelatinous red partial veil, sticky ring, yellow stem above the ring, and red/fibrillose stem below.
cap_surface: Sticky red cap with grayish patches
stem_feature: Sticky ring; yellow above, red/fibrillose below

records.mushroom_card_note: Showy tamarack/larch Suillus with sticky red cap, grayish patches, red gelatinous veil, and a sticky ring.
records.field_identification: Look with tamarack/larch for a sticky red cap with grayish patches, gelatinous red partial veil, sticky ring, yellow stem above the ring, and red/fibrillose stem below.
records.edibility_detail: Usually treated cautiously; distinctive but slimy. Useful as an ID species and possible cooked food with confidence.

records.culinary_uses: This Suillus still needs fuller culinary and host-tree notes.

mushroom_profile.summary: Yellow/orange Suillus that may occur with aspens, oaks, or pine; ringless, glandular-dotted, and often red-streaked.
mushroom_profile.season_note: July through September; summer into fall with aspen or related hardwood habitat where locally present.
research_notes: Needs source links and fuller field notes.
Placeholder image inserted pending real public-usable photo.
Look for yellow cap with red streaks/spots, orange young pores, ringless stem with glandular dots, reddish staining in flesh/stem base, and hardwood/aspen/oak association.

records.mushroom_card_note: Yellow/orange Suillus that may occur with aspens, oaks, or pine; ringless, glandular-dotted, and often red-streaked.
records.field_identification: Look for yellow cap with red streaks/spots, orange young pores, ringless stem with glandular dots, reddish staining in flesh/stem base, and hardwood/aspen/oak association.
records.edibility_detail: Not a beginner food target, but useful to know because it breaks the “Suillus equals conifer” rule.

images_structured.title: Suillus subaureus
images_structured.part_or_stage: fruiting bodies under eastern hemlock / mixed forest context
images_structured.author: Jimmie Veitch
images_structured.credit: Jimmie Veitch, CC BY-SA 3.0, via Wikimedia Commons / Mushroom Observer
images_structured.rights_notes: Commercial use and modification allowed; attribution required and share-alike/copyleft terms may apply to adapted versions.
images_structured.rights_note: Commercial use and modification allowed; attribution required and share-alike/copyleft terms may apply to adapted versions.

records.culinary_uses: Edible reputation exists but texture is modest; better treated as an ID species than a prime food.
records.notes: White Suillus: a pale sticky Suillus strongly associated with eastern white pine. Look for the whitish cap/stem and contrasting glandular dots rather than treating every pale Suillus as this species.

mushroom_profile.summary: White Suillus: eastern white pine associate with bald white cap and conspicuous glandular dots/smears on a slender stem.
staining: May slowly stain pinkish to reddish when sliced
mushroom_profile.season_note: July through October with eastern white pine; northeastern sources describe it as a summer-and-fall Suillus.
research_notes: Needs source links and fuller field notes.
Placeholder image inserted pending real public-usable photo.
Look under eastern white pine for a pale to white cap, slender stem with contrasting glandular dots/smears, no dramatic color staining, and yellowish pores with age.
pore_color: White when very young becoming dull yellow
stem_feature: Slender stem with conspicuous glandular dots/smears

records.general_notes: A white-pine associate; useful in the guide because pale Suillus species can look odd compared with the brown slippery jacks.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Suillus placidus
use_links.notes: White sticky cap, glandular dots, and eastern white pine association.

records.mushroom_card_note: White Suillus: eastern white pine associate with bald white cap and conspicuous glandular dots/smears on a slender stem.
records.field_identification: Look under eastern white pine for a pale to white cap, slender stem with contrasting glandular dots/smears, no dramatic color staining, and yellowish pores with age.
records.edibility_detail: Edible reputation exists but texture is modest; better treated as an ID species than a prime food.

mushroom_profile.summary: White-stemmed Suillus/glandulosipes-type record; ringless or false-veiled yellow/brown Suillus with glandular dots.
mushroom_profile.season_note: July through October with eastern white pine; treated as a summer-into-fall Suillus.
research_notes: Needs source links and fuller field notes.
Placeholder image inserted pending real public-usable photo.
Check for pine or mixed host context, ringless/false-veil characters, yellow to brown cap, yellow pores, and glandular dots. Compare with S. subaureus and S. acidus.
stem_feature: White to pale stem with glandular dots

records.mushroom_card_note: White-stemmed Suillus/glandulosipes-type record; ringless or false-veiled yellow/brown Suillus with glandular dots.
records.field_identification: Check for pine or mixed host context, ringless/false-veil characters, yellow to brown cap, yellow pores, and glandular dots. Compare with S. subaureus and S. acidus.
records.edibility_detail: Keep in review until images and local source support improve.

records.image_coverage_note: Exact Suillus glandulosipes image; do not substitute Suillus placidus for this record.

mushroom_profile.summary: Leccinum-type wrinkled-cap record kept for comparison; needs stronger source and photo support.
mushroom_profile.season_note: July through October; treated with Leccinum-type late-summer and fall timing until local timing is better documented.
research_notes: Needs source links and fuller field notes.
Placeholder image inserted pending real public-usable photo.
Treat as a scabered-stem bolete comparison. Confirm host, cap wrinkling, pore color, scaber pattern, and staining before naming.

records.mushroom_card_note: Leccinum-type wrinkled-cap record kept for comparison; needs stronger source and photo support.
records.field_identification: Treat as a scabered-stem bolete comparison. Confirm host, cap wrinkling, pore color, scaber pattern, and staining before naming.
records.edibility_detail: Not recommended as a food target until species treatment is better supported.

records.culinary_uses: Included as a northern scaber-stalk bolete.

mushroom_profile.summary: Yellow-stemmed Leccinum-type record; useful for comparison but still needs photo/source confirmation.
mushroom_profile.season_note: July through October; treated with Leccinum-type late-summer and fall timing until local timing is better documented.
research_notes: Needs source links and fuller field notes.
Placeholder image inserted pending real public-usable photo.
Look for a yellowish stem with scabers, pore surface, host association, and staining behavior. Do not identify on stem color alone.

records.mushroom_card_note: Yellow-stemmed Leccinum-type record; useful for comparison but still needs photo/source confirmation.
records.field_identification: Look for a yellowish stem with scabers, pore surface, host association, and staining behavior. Do not identify on stem color alone.
records.edibility_detail: Keep as comparison, not a primary edible recommendation.
